title: How to Rent a Boat in Santorini and the Cyclades
tags: boat rental, yacht charter, travel, greece, sailing
topic: How to rent a boat in Santorini and the Cyclades
html:
The Cyclades are one of those rare places that look even better from the water than they do from a postcard. Santorini gets most of the attention, with its caldera cliffs and sunset villages, but the moment you push off from the harbour you realise the whole archipelago is a playground of sea caves, volcanic beaches and quiet coves that the day-trippers never reach. If you have been thinking about spending a day or a week on the Aegean, the simplest way to do it well is to rent a boat and set your own pace. Here is how it actually works, what it costs, and how to avoid the rookie mistakes.

Why a boat beats the crowds

Santorini in high season is busy, and Oia at sunset is shoulder to shoulder. On the water none of that applies. From a boat you swim at the Red Beach and White Beach without the scramble down the cliff path, you anchor off the hot springs near the volcanic islets of Nea Kameni, and you watch the famous sunset from the caldera itself rather than from a packed terrace. Beyond Santorini, the rest of the Cyclades open up: the beaches of Ios, the granite formations of the Small Cyclades, the windswept charm of Naxos and Paros. The freedom to move is the whole point, and it is exactly why a private boat tends to become the highlight of a Greek trip rather than just another excursion.

Choosing the right type of boat

What you book depends on your group, your budget and whether anyone aboard can actually drive. The main options break down like this:

  • Small motorboats and RIBs: Many in Greece can be driven without a licence if the engine is under a certain power. Perfect for a half-day of swimming and beach-hopping near Santorini.
  • Skippered day boats: A local at the helm who knows the currents, the meltemi wind and the best lunch spots. Easily the most relaxing choice.
  • Bareboat sailing yachts: For qualified sailors who want to island-hop over several days. You will need a recognised sailing certificate.
  • Catamarans: Stable, spacious and ideal for families or groups who want shade, room to move and a gentler ride in choppy water.

If you are not certain which suits you, it helps to browse boats by location and read the descriptions before you commit, because a listing tells you fast whether a vessel is built for a lazy swim day or a serious passage.

What it costs and how to budget

Prices swing with the season, the size of the boat and whether a skipper is included. As a rough guide, a small self-drive motorboat for a day sits at the lower end, a skippered RIB or speedboat in the middle, and a crewed catamaran or sailing yacht at the top. Fuel is sometimes extra, so always ask. A few things worth checking before you pay:

  1. Is fuel included or charged on return based on use?
  2. Does the price cover a skipper, or is that an add-on?
  3. What is the deposit, and how is it returned?
  4. Are snorkelling gear, towels and drinks provided?
  5. What happens if the meltemi wind forces a cancellation?

Splitting the cost across a group of six or eight almost always makes a private boat cheaper per head than several separate organised tours, and far more comfortable.

Timing, weather and the meltemi

The Cyclades season runs from late April to October, with July and August the hottest and busiest. The one weather factor you cannot ignore is the meltemi, the strong northerly wind that funnels through the islands in summer. On a calm day the Aegean is glass. On a windy one it can be lumpy and uncomfortable, and some operators will sensibly cancel. Morning departures are usually calmer than afternoons, so if the forecast looks marginal, go early. A good skipper or a reputable rental platform will be honest with you about conditions rather than send you out into a swell.

Book the boat first and the island second. Availability for the best vessels in August disappears weeks ahead, while a quiet guesthouse can almost always be found.

Booking without the headache

The easiest route is to compare listings in one place rather than chasing a dozen local agents over WhatsApp. A marketplace lets you see photos, reviews, prices and what is included side by side, then message the owner directly. When you are ready to hire a boat for your dates, confirm the meeting point, the start time and exactly who is responsible for fuel and the deposit in writing. Screenshot the agreement. It saves any awkwardness on the dock.

One last thought for boat owners

If you happen to own a boat in Greece, or anywhere with a summer charter season, those same travellers are looking for vessels like yours every single day. Putting it in front of them is straightforward: you can list your boat on a worldwide marketplace, set your own availability and earn from the months it would otherwise sit idle in the marina. Whether you are renting for a day or thinking about the other side of the deal, the Cyclades reward anyone willing to head out onto the water. Cast off, find a quiet cove, and let Santorini come to you.
